did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning approach called SkCanNet, which combines a VGG-16 convolutional neural network (CNN) with transfer learning, to classify skin cancer (melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma) from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work proposing a new deep learning model called SkCanNet, which combines VGG-16 with transfer learning. The paper details the methodology, experiments conducted on 1077 dermoscopy images from multiple datasets (MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, HAM10000), and reports specific performance metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity), distinguishing it from a review paper which would primarily summarize prior literature.

title: SkCanNet: A Deep Learning based Skin Cancer Classification Approach
journal: Annals of Emerging Technologies in Computing (AETiC)
brief_summary: This study proposes SkCanNet, a deep learning approach for classifying skin cancer into melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma. The model combines a VGG-16 convolutional neural network with transfer learning and two fully connected layers. It was evaluated on a dataset of 1077 dermoscopy images sourced from MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, and HAM10000. The experimental analysis demonstrates that the proposed model achieves high values for accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity. The results indicate that using pretrained VGG-16 features is an effective method for skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes used in the SkCanNet classification experiment on 1077 dermoscopy images. Described as the deadliest kind of skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion classes (Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes SkCanNet for classifying skin cancer into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and basal cell carcinoma (BCC). The model uses a VGG-16 CNN with transfer learning and fully connected layers to predict these classes from dermoscopy images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity metrics.

datasets_used.name: Combined ISIC Archive Dataset (MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A combined collection of 1077 dermoscopy images sourced from the ISIC archive, specifically comprising subsets MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, and HAM10000. The images were rescaled to 224x224 and augmented using PCA, rotation, random cropping, mirroring, and color-shifting.

use.training: Used for training the SkCanNet model (VGG-16 with transfer learning) after data augmentation.
use.validation: Used for validation to evaluate model performance and check for overfitting.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's classification capabilities.
use.other: The dataset was sourced from the ISIC archive.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity) and comparative tables against other models without statistical significance testing.

pretrained_network_comparison.description: Comparison of various pretrained networks on the combined dataset

Proposed_VGG16_SkCanNet.notes: Achieved saturation at 60th epoch. Outperformed other pretrained networks in terms of accuracy and loss values.

Combined_ISIC_Archive_Dataset_MSK-1_UDA-.notes: The paper reports that the proposed SkCanNet model achieved higher values for accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity. Specific per-class breakdowns for the combined dataset were not explicitly provided in the text, only overall validation accuracy. The model was evaluated on 30% test data.

architecture_used.pretraining: VGG-16 pretrained model (ImageNet weights implied by 'pretrained model' and 'millions of trained parameters'), with original FC layers removed and replaced. Pretrained weights in convolutional layers were frozen during training.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Bottleneck features extracted from the final convolution layer of the frozen VGG-16 backbone

custom_layers.description: Connects bottleneck features to dense layers

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported (described as 'millions of trained parameters' in the pretrained VGG-16 model)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: AlexNet with kNN classifier (Pomponiu et al.)
CNN-based skin cancer diagnosis model with feature extraction
preprocessing_techniques: Rescaling/Resizing: All training images were rescaled to 224x224 pixels to handle varying resolutions and reduce computational expense.
Quality Control/Filtering: Foggy and distant photographs were removed from the test and validation datasets, though they remained in the training set.
Data Augmentation: Applied to the training dataset to prevent overfitting, including PCA (Principal Component Analysis), rotation, random cropping, mirroring, and color-shifting.

conclusions: The proposed SkCanNet model, which combines a VGG-16 convolutional neural network with transfer learning and two fully connected layers, effectively classifies skin cancer into melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and basal cell carcinoma.

The model achieved a validation accuracy of 97.2% on a dataset of 1077 dermoscopy images (sourced from MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, and HAM10000) after 60 epochs, demonstrating high performance in accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.
SkCanNet outperformed other tested pretrained networks (LeNet5, AlexNet, and ResNet) in terms of both accuracy and loss values on the combined dataset.

Using pretrained VGG-16 features via transfer learning is an effective method for skin cancer classification, particularly when dealing with limited dataset sizes, as it reduces training time and yields better accuracy.
The Adam optimizer was found to provide better accuracy compared to the RMSprop optimizer for this specific classification task.

The model was trained without significant overfitting or underfitting, as evidenced by the minimal variation between training and testing accuracy and the minimization of loss through regularization and optimization.

Existing deep learning approaches for skin lesion classification often suffer from drawbacks related to data imbalance, where the number of malignant cases is lower than benign sets, leading to poor model results; the proposed approach aims to mitigate such issues through effective feature extraction.
important_remarks: The dataset is extremely small (1077 images total), which limits the generalizability and statistical robustness of the deep learning model.

Data leakage risk: Foggy and distant photographs were removed from the test and validation sets but remained in the training set, creating a distribution mismatch between training and testing environments.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a combined subset of ISIC archive datasets (MSK-1, UDA-1, UDA-2, HAM10000).
The paper does not report per-class performance metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) for the proposed SkCanNet model, only overall accuracy, specificity, and sensitivity.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was conducted to validate the superiority of SkCanNet over comparative models.
The study focuses only on three classes (Melanoma, SCC, BCC) and excludes other relevant skin lesions or benign conditions present in the source datasets, limiting clinical applicability.
The claim of outperforming other models is based on a limited comparison table (Table 4) with inconsistent reporting of metrics across cited studies, making direct comparison difficult.
The paper acknowledges data imbalance (malignant vs. benign) as a general issue but does not report specific class distribution statistics for their 1077-image dataset.
